questions.question: 0.18 UNIT-IV: Working Capital Management Working Capital operates as the lifeblood of an enterprise. Quantitatively defined as current assets (Gross Working Capital) or current assets minus current liabilities (Net Working Capital) 12, its management requires navigating the precarious equilibrium between liquidity and profitability. Excessive working capital secures the firm against technical insolvency but severely suppresses return on investment, as idle cash and excess inventory yield negligible returns. Conversely, insufficient working capital artificially boosts short-term profitability while exposing the firm to catastrophic supply chain disruptions and insolvency.36 The temporal behavior of working capital delineates into permanent (the irreducible minimum required continuously) and variable (fluctuating with seasonal demands) components.35 How a firm finances these components defines its risk posture. The Hedging (Maturity-Matching) approach aligns the lifespan of assets with their financing source. An Aggressive approach dangerously utilizes short-term liabilities to finance permanent current assets, maximizing profitability at the cost of extreme liquidity risk.12 Working Capital Financing Topologies Evaluation Matrix: UNIT-IV 121. questions.explanation: In financial terminology, gross working capital is synonymous with total current assets.12
